notes.anchor: Let my secretary's hand be a sign to you of my eye-inflammation
notes.note: The letter is in the hand of a scribe (librarius) rather than Cicero's own; he tells Atticus to read the change of hand as proof that his eye-inflammation persists. The same ailment forces the dictation noted in 8.12.

notes.anchor: All our expectation hangs upon the news from Brundisium
notes.note: Pompey was falling back on Brundisium, the chief port for crossing the Adriatic to Greece; everything turned on whether Caesar would trap him there or whether he would get his army across first. Pompey ultimately evacuated to Greece in March 49 BC.

notes.anchor: The country-town men talk much with me
notes.note: The municipales are the propertied men of the Italian country towns near Cicero's villas; he reports that, caring only for their land and savings, they have swung from trusting Pompey to welcoming Caesar. Their shift is Cicero's gauge of collapsing support for the senatorial cause.
